rationale.R1.1: The artifact contains a gap statement ('three directions'), contribution claim ('hybrid index'), and positioning of FastNav — all require verification that the thesis is coherent and the gap is accurately characterized.
rationale.R1.2: The logical chain from prior-work taxonomy → identified gap → proposed method → claimed result must be internally consistent; e.g., the claim that FastNav achieves 'recall of in-memory methods at storage costs of compressed methods' needs to follow from the described mechanism.
rationale.R1.3: The 1.3x speedup claim at same recall@10 on named benchmarks (SIFT1B, Deep1B, BIGANN) is a concrete empirical assertion; a section review must flag whether this claim is backed by presented evidence or is asserted without in-text support.
rationale.R1.4: This is load-bearing: the artifact explicitly positions FastNav against ScaNN, DiskANN, and NSG. Correctness of the novelty claim — that the hybrid index is genuinely new and not subsumed by prior combinations — is central to the section's validity.
rationale.R1.6: Standard prose review applies: sentence-level clarity, precision of terminology (e.g., 'score-aware partitioning', 'navigable structure'), and paragraph cohesion are all in scope for a section review.
rationale.R1.7: Four specific citations are made (Indyk & Motwani 1998, Jegou et al. 2011, Guo et al. 2020, Subramanya et al. 2019, Fu et al. 2019). Accuracy of author lists, years, and attributed claims (e.g., NSG 'theoretical guarantees on navigability') is load-bearing for a literature-heavy related-work section.
